findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify in the plan how `_code_review_prompt_id` maps the incoming `code-review-`-prefixed `prompt_id` back to a logical criterion id for comparison against `effective_criteria(repo_root)` (e.g. strip `code-review-`, restore `project-` → `project.`), mirroring the reverse-mapping in `_criterion_id`.
evidence: Plan: 'consult `registry.effective_criteria(repo_root)` in addition to the static built-in set, so a `project.<name>` code-review criterion resolves'
`effective_criteria` returns e.g. `('project.foo', 'security', ...)` — dotted logical ids, no `code-review-` prefix
`_code_review_prompt_id` receives `prompt_id='code-review-project-foo'` — a `code-review-`-prefixed physical id
Existing analog `_criterion_id` (lines 72-87) performs explicit `prompt_id[len('plan-review-'):]` strip + `project-` → `project.` dot restoration to bridge the same gap for plan-review
findings.finding: The plan says `_code_review_prompt_id` should admit ids present in `registry.effective_criteria(repo_root)`, but `effective_criteria` returns logical criterion ids (e.g. `project.foo`) while `_code_review_prompt_id` receives a `prompt_id` with the `code-review-` prefix (e.g. `code-review-project-foo`). The plan does not specify the reverse-mapping step needed to reconcile these two formats, analogous to the reverse-mapping already implemented for plan-review in `_criterion_id` (eval_solver.py:72-87).
scenarios: If `_code_review_prompt_id` naively checks `prompt_id in effective_criteria(repo_root)`, it always returns `None` for project criteria because `'code-review-project-foo' not in {'project.foo', ...}` — the feature silently does nothing.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a note to the `eval_solver.py` bullet: strip the `code-review-` prefix, restore the `project-` → `project.` dot, and check membership in `effective_criteria(repo_root)` — parallel to the `_criterion_id` logic at lines 72-87.

findings.finding: `_code_review_prompt_id` is extended to check `registry.effective_criteria(repo_root)`, but `effective_criteria()` returns LOGICAL criterion ids (e.g. `project.foo`), while `_code_review_prompt_id` receives PHYSICAL prompt ids (e.g. `code-review-project-foo`). The plan does not specify how the physical id is matched against the logical-id vocabulary, leaving an implicit vocabulary mismatch that would cause the guard to always return `None` for project criteria.
findings.location: Approach section — `eval_solver.py` bullet: 'consult `registry.effective_criteria(repo_root)` … in addition to the static built-in set'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the exact gate-membership discriminator in calibrate_criterion's new branch (e.g., 'check `registry.effective_criteria(repo_root, gate_key="code_review")`' or 'probe `criterion_prompt_id(criterion_id, gate_key="code_review")` and check for the `code-review-` prefix') so an implementer cannot use a heuristic that mis-routes plan-review project criteria.
evidence: Plan says 'add a code-review branch that resolves the physical prompt id via criterion_prompt_id(criterion_id, gate_key="code_review") — the gate-keyed parameter added by child 1 — when the id belongs to the code-review gate'

The HOW of gate-membership detection is absent: consulting registry.effective_criteria(repo_root, gate_key='code_review') vs. a prefix check vs. probing the routing index are three distinct mechanisms with different correctness properties
The plan already specifies that eval_solver.py's extension should 'consult registry.effective_criteria(repo_root)' — but calibrate_criterion's branch condition is left unspecified
ABSENCE: no statement of which function/data-structure is consulted to classify a criterion_id as code-review vs. plan-review in calibrate_criterion
findings.finding: The plan does not specify the discriminator used in calibrate_criterion's new code-review branch to determine that a criterion_id belongs to the code-review gate (vs. the plan-review gate). 'When the id belongs to the code-review gate' is stated as the branch condition but the mechanism is left implicit — an implementer could use a heuristic (e.g., checking for a 'project.' prefix) that would mis-route a project plan-review criterion through the code-review arm if both gates have project criteria activated.
findings.location: Approach section — eval.py calibrate_criterion description; also AC line 1
scenarios: A project activates both a plan-review criterion (project.bar) and a code-review criterion (project.foo); an implementer using a 'project.' prefix check routes project.bar through the code-review arm, causing a wrong eval path
A future built-in code-review criterion with a non-project id would need the same gate-membership check, and an under-specified discriminator makes that extension fragile
findings.suggested_fix: In the Approach section, add: 'Gate-membership detection in calibrate_criterion uses `registry.effective_criteria(repo_root)` from `rebar.llm.code_review.registry` — the same call used in eval_solver.py — to decide whether criterion_id is a code-review id before taking the code-review branch; alternatively, resolve via `criterion_prompt_id(criterion_id, gate_key="code_review")` and check whether the result starts with `"code-review-"`.' Either phrasing is sufficient; the key is that it mirrors the eval_solver.py discriminator for consistency.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define the gate-detection predicate in the Approach (e.g., 'check `criterion_id in registry.effective_criteria(repo_root)` from `code_review.registry`, falling back to the plan-review branch') so the branching logic in `calibrate_criterion` is unambiguous.
evidence: Plan text: 'add a code-review branch that resolves the physical prompt id via `criterion_prompt_id(criterion_id, gate_key="code_review")` — … when the id belongs to the code-review gate'
No decision rule is stated for how membership in the code-review gate is tested

`registry.effective_criteria(repo_root)` (code-review) and `plan_review.registry.by_id(repo_root)` (plan-review) both exist and could be used, but neither is designated as the gate-detection mechanism
findings.finding: The gate-detection predicate — the decision rule `calibrate_criterion` uses to decide whether a `criterion_id` belongs to the code-review gate vs. the plan-review gate — is never specified. The plan says 'when the id belongs to the code-review gate' but gives no concrete rule (registry lookup, prefix test, explicit flag, etc.).
scenarios: A built-in plan-review criterion id that happens to also appear in the code-review registry would be routed incorrectly if the predicate is a simple registry membership check without gate disambiguation
An implementer using a prefix heuristic (`project.` prefix → code-review) would break for project plan-review criteria, which also use `project.`

findings.finding: `_fired` in `calibrate_criterion` checks `verdict.get('findings')`, but a code-review verifier case (`code-review-verify`) returns `{verifications:[...]}`, not `{findings:[...]}`. The plan states scoring is 'findings presence (recall / no-fire) or verification presence' but never specifies which `_fired` variant applies in the calibration loop for a verifier-type eval spec, leaving the fire-detection protocol for verifier cases undefined.
findings.location: eval.py:379-381 (`_fired`); Approach section — '_run_code_review_case' output schema description

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Name the concrete exception class (e.g., `CriterionResolutionError`) that must be raised for a missing eval spec, and define 'located' (e.g., 'the exception message includes the criterion id string and the file/line of the resolver call') in AC3 and the Testing edge-case bullet.
evidence: 'raises a **located** error naming the criterion (does not silently pass or emit a generic error); verified by... asserting the raised error type and that the message contains the criterion id'
The plan does not name the exception class anywhere in Approach, Scope, Testing, or AC text.
'Located error' appears in the plan twice but is never defined.
findings.finding: The exception/error type for the 'located error' in AC3 is never named. The plan says 'asserting the raised error type' but specifies no class (e.g., ValueError, a custom CriterionNotFoundError, etc.), leaving the executor to invent a contract that is never specified. 'Located error' is internal jargon whose concrete form (exception class, field name, message structure) is undefined.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria, AC3 / Testing, 'Edge (missing spec)' bullet
scenarios: An executor writing the test must invent an exception class; another executor reviewing the AC cannot verify the right type was used; a future reader cannot distinguish a ValueError from a domain-specific exception without reading the source.
findings.suggested_fix: Add one sentence to the 'Edge (missing spec)' testing bullet: 'The raised exception is `CriterionResolutionError` (or `ValueError` if no custom class is introduced), and its message contains the criterion id string (e.g., `project.foo`).' Update AC3 to match: '...raises a `CriterionResolutionError` whose message contains the criterion id.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the error class (e.g., `EvalError`) and the minimum message content (e.g., must contain the logical `criterion_id`, not just the physical `prompt_id`) for the missing-spec located error.
evidence: Plan: 'A project criterion with no eval spec raises a **located** error naming the criterion.'
AC: 'verified by the same `-k eval` selector asserting the raised error type and that the message contains the criterion id'
`load_eval_spec` already raises `EvalError` with the prompt_id (not the criterion_id) — so a new wrapping raise or re-raise with criterion_id is needed, but the error class is unspecified
findings.finding: The error class/type for the 'located error naming the criterion' (missing eval spec for a project code-review criterion) is never specified in the plan body, but the AC requires the test to assert 'the raised error type'.
findings.location: Approach section, third bullet / AC third item
scenarios: Implementer raises a plain `ValueError` naming the criterion; test asserts `EvalError` — test fails
Implementer relies on the existing `EvalError` from `load_eval_spec` (which names the prompt_id, not the criterion_id) — AC's 'message contains the criterion id' assertion fails
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the Approach: 'Raise `EvalError` with a message containing the logical `criterion_id` (e.g., `project.foo`) — not just the physical prompt id — so the AC assertion on message content is unambiguous.'

That call site must also be updated to pass `repo_root`, yet the Scope section only mentions changes to the function definition — the call-site update is unenumerated.

evidence: Plan states 'consult registry.effective_criteria(repo_root) (exists at src/rebar/llm/code_review/registry.py:179-184)' — new import coupling from evals to code_review package
Plan states 'add a code-review branch that resolves the physical prompt id via criterion_prompt_id(criterion_id, gate_key="code_review")' — eval surface becomes gate-aware
No mention of ADR, AGENTS.md, or design-doc update anywhere in the plan
The 'Rejected alternative' section provides inline justification but is not a durable architectural record
findings.finding: The plan introduces a new cross-component dependency (evals → code_review registry) and makes the eval surface gate-aware — a meaningful architectural decision — but no ADR, AGENTS.md, or design-doc update is planned to capture it.
scenarios: A future developer extending the eval surface to a third gate (e.g., 'docs_review') finds no documented pattern or decision record explaining why the gate-keyed resolver approach was chosen over a separate entrypoint.
Onboarding review misses that evals now couple to the code_review registry, creating an unexpected import dependency that could be broken by code_review refactors.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a brief ADR entry or AGENTS.md section under the evals component documenting: (1) eval resolution is gate-aware, (2) code-review criteria are resolved via `code_review.registry.effective_criteria(repo_root)`, (3) the rejected alternative (separate entrypoint) and why it was rejected.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Verify that child ticket `[rebar:fc72-bf7e-7652-4228]` concretely adds a `gate_key` parameter to `criterion_prompt_id` (or an equivalent gate-dispatching helper), and document the exact new signature/module location in this plan's Approach section so the dependency is independently verifiable.
evidence: Plan states: 'resolves the physical prompt id via `criterion_prompt_id(criterion_id, gate_key="code_review")`  — the gate-keyed parameter added by child 1 `[rebar:fc72-bf7e-7652-4228]`'
Actual function signature: `def criterion_prompt_id(criterion_id: str) -> str:` — single parameter, no `gate_key`
Function body: `return f"{PLAN_REVIEW_PROMPT_PREFIX}{criterion_id.replace('.', '-')}"` — always emits `plan-review-` prefix regardless of gate
Ticket `rebar:fc72-bf7e-7652-4228` returned: 'Ticket not found' — dependency is unverifiable
No codebase evidence of a gate-keyed variant of `criterion_prompt_id` anywhere under `src/rebar/llm`
findings.finding: The plan's `calibrate_criterion` code-review branch relies on `criterion_prompt_id(criterion_id, gate_key="code_review")` — a gate-keyed overload attributed to child ticket `[rebar:fc72-bf7e-7652-4228]`. The actual `criterion_prompt_id` in `src/rebar/llm/criteria/ids.py` accepts only one argument (`criterion_id: str`) and unconditionally prepends `plan-review-`, so calling it with `gate_key="code_review"` would raise `TypeError`, and even if that were silently ignored it would produce `plan-review-project-foo` instead of `code-review-project-foo`. The dependency ticket is not resolvable in the system.
scenarios: When `calibrate_criterion` is called with a `project.foo` code-review criterion id, `criterion_prompt_id('project.foo', gate_key='code_review')` raises TypeError (unexpected keyword argument), blocking the entire branch.

Even if the call were restructured to avoid TypeError, the current function returns `plan-review-project-foo` causing `load_eval_spec` to look for a plan-review eval file rather than a code-review eval file, silently loading the wrong spec or raising a misleading EvalError.
findings.suggested_fix: In the Approach section, replace the vague reference to 'the gate-keyed parameter added by child 1' with the exact new signature, e.g.: 'child 1 adds `criterion_prompt_id(criterion_id, gate_key="code_review") -> str` that emits `code-review-project-<name>` for project criteria in the code-review gate; this ticket calls that overload.' Alternatively, if the gate dispatch lives in a separate function (e.g., `code_review_criterion_prompt_id`), name that function explicitly so reviewers can probe it.

findings.finding: The plan's `calibrate_criterion` code-review branch calls `criterion_prompt_id(criterion_id, gate_key="code_review")`, but the current `criterion_prompt_id` signature (`src/rebar/llm/criteria/ids.py:36-42`) accepts only `criterion_id` and always prefixes with `plan-review-` — no `gate_key` parameter exists.
